name: City of Los Angeles v. Patel
summary: A Los Angeles ordinance required hotel operators to make guest registry records immediately available for police inspection on demand, with criminal penalties for refusal and no mechanism to seek pre-compliance judicial review. The Court held the ordinance facially unconstitutional because it failed to provide any opportunity for pre-compliance review before a search could occur.
holding: A statute authorizing warrantless searches of business records is facially unconstitutional when it provides no opportunity for the subject of the search to obtain pre-compliance judicial review. Hotels are not so pervasively regulated that they fall within the closely regulated industry exception, and their operators retain Fourth Amendment protections against unreasonable inspections.

legal_definitions.definition: An opportunity for the party subject to an administrative inspection demand to seek judicial review of the demand before being required to comply, thereby preserving Fourth Amendment protections against unreasonable searches.
legal_definitions.context: The Court held that administrative subpoenas and inspection regimes must afford a meaningful opportunity for pre-compliance judicial review to satisfy the Fourth Amendment.

relevance_to_audit: Establishes that mandatory disclosure ordinances for business records must afford pre-compliance review; relevant to government data demands, surveillance registry requirements, and administrative search programs.

key_quotes.text: Absent an opportunity for precompliance review, the ordinance creates an intolerable risk that searches authorized by it will exceed statutory limits, or be used as a pretext to harass hotel operators and their guests.
key_quotes.context: Explaining why the absence of pre-compliance judicial review renders the ordinance constitutionally defective.

procedural_posture: Ninth Circuit affirmed permanent injunction; affirmed
subsequent_treatment: Applied to digital records disclosure demands and surveillance data retention ordinances by lower courts
